<!-- Generated by a multi-agent review (16 subsystem/cross-cutting lenses, each finding adversarially verified). 104 raw findings -> 47 confirmed. Reviewed on macOS arm64, Python 3.13, before first live-camera bring-up. -->

# PalletScan Pre-Live Review — Final Report

## Executive Summary

This is a genuinely high-quality codebase. The threading model is disciplined (single-writer counters, generation-fenced readers, documented stale-connection guards), the failure semantics are deliberate and self-documenting (warn-vs-fail policy, exit-code escalation, at-least-once outbox, "events are the product"), and almost every macOS quirk has a written assumption and an arrival-day checklist behind it. Most adversarially-tested "bugs" turned out to be correct-by-design — that is a strong signal. The single most important thing to fix before plugging in cameras is the **unbounded `cap.read()` in `measure_achieved_fps` on the connect/reopen path**: on the never-before-exercised AVFoundation backend, a wedged or settling device can hang the first live connect with no signal handler installed yet, requiring a SIGKILL — and it also defeats the watchdog's autonomous recovery. Beyond that, the watchdog's default escalation valves are effectively off (`max_outage_s=None`), so a camera that opens-but-delivers-nothing can scan zero pallets indefinitely while everything looks healthy. Confidence on the purely macOS/AVFoundation items (fps readback, format negotiation, exposure semantics, read-blocking) is necessarily provisional until a real See3CAM is attached — those are flagged explicitly below.

---

## Critical

_None. No finding rises to data-corruption-on-the-default-path with certainty; the highest-impact items are High._

---

## High

**1. `measure_achieved_fps` can hang the connect/reopen path forever on a blocking `cap.read()`**
`palletscan/sources/controls.py:271-292`
Both the warmup loop and the sampling loop call a blocking `cap.read()` with no per-read timeout; the time bound is only checked *between* reads. On AVFoundation a read can block waiting for a frame from a wedged or just-settling device — exactly the first-run scenario. This runs on every connect (`connect_verify_s=1.0` default) and every watchdog reopen, so a hang here freezes the source thread inside connect and the watchdog cannot recover via its normal sample-timeout path. Worse, on the very first live connect the source is built *before* signal handlers are installed (`cli.py`), so a native blocked read defers Ctrl-C and may need SIGKILL.
**Fix:** Compute a hard wall-clock deadline (`t0 + warmup_budget + sample_s`) that breaks the warmup loop too, and cap consecutive read failures so the function returns early with `frames=0` (a failed verify the watchdog can act on) instead of hanging. Rely on `close()`/`release()` to unblock an already-wedged read.

**2. Inline pyzbar QR decode runs with no timeout on the full-resolution motion ROI**
`palletscan/pipeline/decode_engine.py:178-190`
The cascade's remaining-budget check and early break guard only the DATAMATRIX branch; QR (pyzbar) always runs to completion with no internal time cap. On a 2064×1552@72fps (~14ms budget) or 1920×1200@120fps (~8ms budget) camera, a large ROI QR scan can take tens of ms, deterministically exceeding `frame_budget_ms` (default 50ms is already larger than the inter-frame interval). The single pipeline thread stalls per active frame, the `DroppingQueue(maxsize=64)` overflows and silently drops frames, and under burst conditions the dropped frames can include the only decodable ones of a pass — threatening "every pass accounted for." Never observed because tests ran on small synthetic crops.
**Fix:** Downscale large ROIs for the QR fast path (cap the longer side to ~1000px and map the rect back), and/or skip QR when remaining budget is below a measured per-crop cost. Feed measured pyzbar wall time into the deadline math so the DM/fan-out steps see the real remaining budget.

**3. Motion debounce thresholds are frame-counts, not time — one pass splits into many at native fps**
`palletscan/pipeline/motion_gate.py:109,126`
`open_frames` (default 3) and `quiet_frames` (default 8) are consumed as raw consecutive-frame counts, tuned against the 30fps synthetic source. At 120fps, `quiet_frames=8` closes a segment after only 67ms of "quiet" (111ms at 72fps). A normal blank pallet face or slat presenting a low-texture region for >67ms splits one physical pass into two candidates — overstating decode attribution, minting spurious MISSes, and corrupting A/B comparison (the two arms run at different fps, so the same pass debounces differently). The system already knows each source's `nominal_fps` but deliberately does not use it here.
**Fix:** Make debounce time-based: store `open_s`/`quiet_s` in `MotionConfig` and convert to frame counts per-source via `source.nominal_fps` inside `MotionGate` (`open_frames = max(1, round(open_s * fps))`, etc.). At minimum, set per-camera overrides (≈`open_frames 12`, `quiet_frames 30` at 120fps) and validate them against real fps before going live.

---

## Medium

**4. Watchdog spins forever on a persistent geometry/layout mismatch with no escalation**
`palletscan/sources/camera.py:327-362`
`_verify_frame_shape` raises `CameraReadError` on a width/height mismatch or undecodable layout. The watchdog treats this as a recoverable error and reopens with the *identical* locked config. If AVFoundation persistently snaps the configured 2064×1552/1920×1200 mode to a supported geometry, every reopen re-delivers a mismatched first frame and re-raises — an infinite reopen→fail loop. Because the reader exits cleanly (not stuck in `read()`), `max_zombie_readers` never trips; `max_outage_s` is off by default. The camera is permanently down with healthy-looking counters and the A/B arm silently at zero. (Depends on real hardware actually snapping the geometry — confirmable only with a camera attached.)
**Fix:** Raise a distinct *fatal* exception from `_verify_frame_shape` that the watchdog escalates to `WatchdogEscalation` (exit 3) instead of retrying an identical config, or cap consecutive reopens that never yield a frame and escalate. Re-applying a mode the device refuses cannot succeed by repetition.

**5. Supervisor cannot detect a wedged-but-alive child; default escalation valves are off**
`palletscan/reliability/supervisor.py:398-406, 419-487` (with `palletscan/config.py:523`)
The supervisor acts only on child *exit* or an explicit stop. A child that is alive but hung — e.g. the watchdog in a reopen-succeeds-but-no-frames loop — keeps `poll()==None` forever and the supervisor waits forever, reporting the station healthy while it scans nothing. The in-process valves do not cover this: `max_zombie_readers` only fires on threads stuck in `read()` (a clean reopen leaves no zombie), and `max_outage_s` defaults to `None`. This is the most direct way the core invariant silently fails. (Most plausible exactly on the untested AVFoundation path, where a device may open/enumerate but not deliver frames.)
**Fix:** (1) Ship a sane non-None default for `watchdog.max_outage_s` (e.g. 120s) so a no-frames-after-reopen loop escalates to exit 3 and a real restart. (2) Optionally add a supervisor-side heartbeat (child touches a file, or supervisor reads the metrics endpoint). At minimum, document that `max_outage_s` MUST be set for production.

**6. `acquire()` treats every `flock` OSError as "lock held," masking unsupported-filesystem errors on macOS**
`palletscan/reliability/instance_lock.py:116-120`
The bare `except OSError` converts *any* flock failure into `InstanceLockHeld` → exit 4 ("another instance holds … stop it first"). Genuine contention is `BlockingIOError` (errno 35); flock on an unsupported volume returns ENOTSUP(45)/EOPNOTSUPP(102), and NFS-without-lockd can return ENOLCK(77). If the owner points `--data-dir` at an external/network/FUSE volume (common on a fresh dev machine), they get a confusing "another instance running" message with no real instance — chasing a phantom process instead of the I/O error. (Does not fire on a default local APFS data dir.)
**Fix:** Narrow the contention catch to `except BlockingIOError`; let other OSErrors propagate or wrap them with a distinct message ("cannot lock `<path>`: `<err>` (network/FUSE volume?)").

**7. ProcessPool variant path re-pickles the crop per variant and cold-loads decoders per worker on macOS spawn**
`palletscan/pipeline/decode_engine.py:81-110, 205-208`
In `ExecutorKind.PROCESS` mode, each fallback frame submits 5 variant tasks, pickling the full ROI to a worker 5× per frame. macOS defaults to `spawn`, so each worker is a fresh interpreter that must import cv2/pyzbar/pylibdmtx and build `_task_decoders()` (per-process `lru_cache`) on first use — seconds of first-fallback latency. If a worker's dylib import fails, `fut.result()` re-raises and is swallowed per-frame (`frame_errors++`), so fallback silently never works in process mode. **This is opt-in** — the default `THREAD` executor avoids all of it — so it only bites if the owner sets `executor: process`.
**Fix:** For PROCESS mode, submit the crop once and let one task try all variants, and use a pool `initializer` to import decoders/dylibs at worker startup (warm pool). Document that PROCESS mode needs the dylib path available in the spawned env (DYLD/Homebrew paths may not survive spawn under SIP).

**8. Variant fan-out tasks keep running after a winner is found, leaking budget and worker capacity**
`palletscan/pipeline/decode_engine.py:205-229`
On an early hit the function returns and the `finally` calls `fut.cancel()`, but `cancel()` cannot stop already-running C calls. With `workers=2` and 5 variants, the 2 in-flight tasks (each running uncapped pyzbar + capped libdmtx) run to completion past the deadline, occupying both worker threads. The next stubborn frame submits 5 more behind the stragglers, growing the executor queue and degrading fallback latency precisely when a pass is hard to read. The libdmtx call is budget-capped, so a single straggler's overshoot is bounded, but worker occupancy is not.
**Fix:** Give each variant task its own wall budget checked inside `_variant_task`, cap pyzbar input (downscale), and submit budget-aware waves when `workers < variant count`. Account in-flight straggler time so `budget_overruns` reflects reality.

**9. Rolling buffer holds full-resolution frames — ~1.5–1.7 GB per camera, doubled in A/B mode**
`palletscan/pipeline/rolling_buffer.py:18-26`
The buffer retains full-res grayscale frames for the whole horizon. Color cam: ~750 frames × 2.30 MB ≈ 1.7 GB; mono cam: ~512 × 3.20 MB ≈ 1.6 GB. In A/B station mode both run at once (~2.5–3.4 GB just for evidence buffers), atop the decode executor and queues. On a single dev box this risks paging/OOM during the first live run, which manifests as frame drops/watchdog stalls and thus missed accounting. Bounded by design, but the bound is large and undocumented at real frame rates (the "flat memory" soak ran at small synthetic resolutions).
**Fix:** Bound the buffer by total bytes (track cumulative `nbytes`, evict to a memory cap) and/or keep a downscaled copy for evidence, full-res only for the active segment. At minimum, log the computed footprint at startup so the owner sees ~1.7 GB/camera before it bites.

**10. `upsert_camera_yaml` fsyncs nothing before/after `os.replace` — a crash can leave a truncated config**

> _Severity note: verifier downgraded this to **Low** (rare, interactive-only, recoverable via .bak). Folded into Low section below._

**11. `current_mode()`/`choose_mode()` ranking breaks on AVFoundation because the FOURCC readback is garbage**
`palletscan/sources/probe.py:101-108, 186-191`
`choose_mode` classifies uncompressed-vs-MJPG via the FOURCC *readback* (`cap.get(CAP_PROP_FOURCC)`). On AVFoundation this returns 0 → `'????'`, which is never in `UNCOMPRESSED_FOURCCS`, so the "prefer uncompressed over MJPG among near-equal modes" tie-break silently no-ops and MJPG (whose compression artifacts hurt Data Matrix decode) can be locked over a near-equal raw mode. The live path already falls back to the *configured* fourcc on an unreadable readback, but the probe path has no equivalent fallback. (macOS-only; the Windows production path reports truthful FOURCC. Confirm readback truthfulness once a See3CAM is attached — see ARRIVAL_CHECKLIST.)
**Fix:** When the readback is unverifiable, classify using the *requested* candidate fourcc (`cand.fourcc`) instead of the readback, mirroring the live path. Track a `fourcc_readback_trusted` flag.

**12. CSV formula injection: scanned barcode payloads written unsanitized as the leading CSV cell**
`palletscan/reporting/render.py:109-119`
`reconciliation_csv()` writes the decoded payload (arbitrary external data read off a pallet) as the first cell via `csv.writer`, which does RFC-4180 quoting but does *not* neutralize spreadsheet formula injection. A payload beginning with `=`/`+`/`-`/`@` (e.g. `=HYPERLINK(...)`) becomes a live formula when an operator opens the downloaded report in Excel/LibreOffice on the factory PC. (The `ab_csv` `camera` column is config-controlled, so that half is low-risk; exploitation also needs a crafted barcode scanned *and* a human to open the CSV and accept the prompt.)
**Fix:** Prefix-quote any external text cell that starts with `=+-@` or a control char (`\t\r\n`), e.g. `lambda v: "'"+v if isinstance(v,str) and v[:1] in "=+-@\t\r\n" else v`. Apply to `payload`.

**13. `close()` returns while a watchdog reopen is still driving the device (shutdown re-acquires a stopped camera)**
`palletscan/reliability/watchdog.py:179-195, 237-294`
`close()` sets `_stop`, closes the inner source, drains the handoff, and joins only the *reader* (1.0s) — never the consumer thread, which is the one running `_recover()` → `reopen()` → `_connect()` → `measure_achieved_fps` for `connect_verify_s`. `measure_achieved_fps` loops on wall-time only with no `_closed`/`_stop` check, so on the reopen path the device is re-opened and exercised for the full ~1s during a shutdown the operator believes finished; `app.run()`'s `source_t.join()` has no timeout and blocks for that whole time. The post-reopen `_stop` guard fires only *after* reopen fully returns. (Narrow race window; bounded latency; device is always ultimately released, so no leak.)
**Fix:** Make `measure_achieved_fps`/connect-verify abort-aware (pass a `should_stop` callable, break early), and re-check `_stop` between `_connect` sub-steps. Optionally skip connect-verify entirely on the watchdog reopen path. Also add `if self._stop.is_set(): return` immediately before the `inner.reopen()` call.

**14. A hung native `VideoCapture` open during reopen can hang the whole station; supervisor can't auto-recover**
`palletscan/app.py:326-335, 444-483` (with `palletscan/sources/camera.py` reopen path)
`app.run()` joins `source_t`/`pipeline_t` with no timeout; the only exit is the source generator returning. During a watchdog reopen, `reopen()` sets `self._cap = None` *before* the raw `cv2.VideoCapture(index, backend)` constructor returns, so a wedged constructor cannot be interrupted by `close()` (which would release `None`). On macOS, AVFoundation device-open can hang on first access (permission prompt, device-busy). If camera A escalates while camera B is mid-construction, B's source thread never returns, the station hangs forever on join, and the supervisor (which restarts only on *exit*) cannot recover — only an operator kill ends it. (Requires the narrow race of B's constructor being wedged at the moment A escalates; first-ever macOS run is where it's most plausible.)
**Fix:** Bound the `source_t`/`StationRunner` joins with a timeout; on timeout with `_stop` set, proceed to executor/bus shutdown and raise, accepting the daemon source thread as leaked-but-not-blocking. Document that a hung native open on macOS leaks a thread until process exit and that supervisor restart (exit 3) is the real recovery.

---

## Low

**JsonlSink flushes but never fsyncs — JSONL tail lost on power failure.** `palletscan/events/sinks.py:65-71` — `handle()` does `write()`+`flush()` but no `os.fsync`; a hard power loss on the factory box loses the most recent `events.jsonl` lines. SqliteSink (WAL commit) and the HTTP outbox are the durable record by design, so the core invariant holds; this is a defense-in-depth gap. **Fix:** `os.fsync(self._file.fileno())` after flush (optionally batched), or document JSONL as best-effort.

**Outbox size cap counts characters, not bytes.** `palletscan/events/http_sink.py:164-193` — `SUM(LENGTH(body))` is a character count and ignores per-row/btree/WAL overhead, so `outbox.db` can exceed `max_mb`. Overshoot is modest for ASCII barcode payloads (char==byte) and the WAL is auto-checkpoint-bounded, so "fill the disk" is unlikely. **Fix:** Use `SUM(LENGTH(CAST(body AS BLOB)))` plus a per-row overhead estimate, or bound on actual `PRAGMA page_count*page_size` (+ the `-wal` file); document what `max_mb` means.

**`EvidenceWriter.prune` re-scans the whole evidence tree on every miss.** `palletscan/events/evidence.py:183-209` — `prune()` runs per `write_burst` and `rglob`+`stat`s every surviving dir; with a full 500MB tree this is O(total files) of syscalls on the pipeline thread per miss, which can stall the consumer of the (dropping) live-frame queue under a high miss rate. **Fix:** Memoize per-dir sizes (each dir is write-once), or read `meta.json` frame_count; skip rescanning the just-written keep dir.

**`import_pylibdmtx()` runs at module import in `sources/render.py`.** `palletscan/sources/render.py:18` — eager `pylibdmtx = import_pylibdmtx()` at module top; `synthetic.py` imports `render` at module level, so importing the synthetic source (or `load_truth_jsonl` for replay reconcile) hard-fails at import if the libdmtx dylib isn't loadable — the exact "best-effort dev" macOS scenario — even when no DM rendering is needed. Loud, actionable RuntimeError (not silent). **Fix:** Defer to a lazy module global initialized in the first function that renders a Data Matrix, mirroring `PylibdmtxDecoder`.

**Y16 mono down-shift hardcodes `>>8` (MSB-justified) assumption.** `palletscan/sources/video.py:76-77` — `(img >> 8)` keeps the high byte; wrong for right-justified 12-bit Y16. **Not on the owner's path:** the 37CUGM auto-calibrates to GREY (8-bit), and `convert_rgb=True` default yields BGR — Y16 requires a deliberate manual opt-in (`fourcc: Y16` + `convert_rgb: false`). **Fix:** Make the down-shift per-camera configurable, or auto-detect which 8-bit window carries the dynamic range; log mean/max of the down-shifted plane at connect.

**Re-calibrating with `--name` on an existing camera id wipes its saved settings/backend.** `palletscan/calibrate.py:95-101` — the `--name` branch builds a fresh `CameraConfig` (defaults: `exposure_auto=True`, `backend=AUTO`, etc.), discarding any existing entry with that id, then upserts by id. The safe re-calibrate path is `--camera <id>` *without* `--name` (preserves all fields). Footgun because the day-one bootstrap message teaches the `--name` form. **Fix:** On the `--name` branch, look up an existing entry by id and `model_copy(update={"name": ...})` onto it; only build a fresh config when none exists.

**Config fields lacking lower-bound validation.** `palletscan/config.py:198-211, 184-196, 165-176` — `dedup.window_s`, `buffer.pre_s/post_s`, `decode.workers/frame_budget_ms`, `motion.open_frames/quiet_frames`, etc. accept zero/negative silently (unlike sibling configs). The concrete crash is `decode.workers=0` → `ValueError` deep in `ThreadPoolExecutor` (exit 1, bypassing the clean exit-2 config path). (`frame_stride` and `confirmations` are already guarded by `max(1, …)` downstream, contrary to the original claim.) **Fix:** Add `field_validator`s: `workers>=1`, `window_s>0`, `pre_s/post_s>=0`, `frame_budget_ms>0`, `jpeg_quality in 1..100`, `open_frames/quiet_frames>=1`.

**`upsert_camera_yaml` does not fsync tmp file or parent dir around `os.replace`.** `palletscan/config.py:654-666` — a hard crash between rename and writeback can leave a zero-length/partial config. Interactive `calibrate --save` only (not the 24/7 hot path), with a `.bak` fallback. **Fix:** `fh.flush(); os.fsync(fh.fileno())` before close, then `os.fsync` the parent dir after `os.replace` (wrapped in try/except).

**Same-second calibrate saves collide on the `.bak` filename.** `palletscan/config.py:647-653` — second-granularity `strftime` stamp; two saves in one second overwrite the prior backup. Implausible interactively. **Fix:** Sub-second granularity (`time.time_ns`/`%f`) or refuse to overwrite an existing `.bak`.

**Disk self-test over-reserves on multi-volume setups.** `palletscan/selftest.py:330-364` — requires `2×(evidence.max_mb + http.max_mb)` free on *each* volume even when evidence and outbox are on different filesystems. Fails safe (only over-reserves) but can hard-FAIL a well-provisioned station with a confusing message on arrival day. **Fix:** Per-volume, charge only that volume's own cap; if both share `st_dev`, sum and check the shared volume once.

**Rotating JSONL log timestamps are naive local time, no UTC offset.** `palletscan/logging_setup.py:28-37` — diagnostic log `ts` uses `time.localtime` with no zone designator while business events use UTC+offset; harder to eyeball-correlate, ambiguous across DST fall-back. Consistent with the `restarts.jsonl` audit log and lock file (which are also naive-local), and no correctness path consumes it — diagnostics only. **Fix:** Emit UTC with an explicit offset to match the event store.

**Child log-prune can unlink the supervisor's open `supervisor.jsonl` on macOS.** `palletscan/logging_setup.py:96-119` — `prune_old_logs` spares only `restarts.jsonl`; the child prunes the shared log dir, and on POSIX unlinking an open file succeeds, so a `supervisor.jsonl` older than `max_age_days` is silently destroyed while the supervisor keeps writing to the unlinked inode. Requires a multi-week quiet window; loses diagnostics, not scan correctness. **Fix:** Spare `supervisor.jsonl` and `supervisor.jsonl.*` by name, or isolate the supervisor log in a subdir the child never prunes.

**Periodic stats thread has no exception guard.** `palletscan/app.py:440-442` — `_stats_loop` runs `snapshot()` (which invokes queue/gauge/outbox-probe callables) with no try/except in a daemon thread; one raise (e.g. a non-numeric `MIN(enqueued_utc)` in `outbox_stats` at `http_sink.py:320`, outside its `sqlite3.Error` guard) silently kills all further metrics lines for the rest of a 24/7 run. **Fix:** Wrap the loop body in try/except, log, and continue.

**Station thread-start partial failure orphans started runner threads.** `palletscan/station.py:251-260` — if `thread.start()` raises for the Nth thread, the join loop is skipped and the started daemon runners keep publishing while `business_bus.shutdown()` runs; events after the SENTINEL are logged+counted (not silent) and the run already fails (the start exception propagates after the `finally`). Residual: the started threads are never stopped/joined. Narrow (≤2 cameras, needs OS thread exhaustion). **Fix:** Track which threads started; on failure call `self.stop()` and join the started threads before draining the bus.

**Per-camera `RunSummary` never surfaces `events_lost`/`published_after_shutdown`.** `palletscan/app.py:490-501` — `run()` fails on `not drained` but never inspects `published_after_shutdown`, and the summary omits both counters. Structurally unreachable today (shutdown happens after joins), so a latent one-sided safety net. **Fix:** Treat `published_after_shutdown > 0` as a run failure and add both counters to `RunSummary`.

**`ReadStore._connect()` never sets `journal_mode=WAL`.** `palletscan/web/store.py:60-64` — the module docstring relies on "WAL + busy_timeout," but the dashboard creates the DB in default rollback mode before the SqliteSink flips it to WAL. In the brief window before the first event commit, a dashboard write can block the sink's commit past `busy_timeout` and raise SQLITE_BUSY — but the bus catches it (`sink_errors++`, continues), so it's a rare, logged, recoverable single-row drop in the dashboard store, not a pipeline crash. **Fix:** Add `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL` to `ReadStore._connect()` (idempotent; whichever connection opens first establishes WAL).

**Dashboard `_detail()` can 500 on an unparseable `detail_json` row.** `palletscan/web/store.py:66-69` — `json.loads` with no guard, mapped over every row in `recent_events`/`misses`/`pass_and_miss_rows`, contradicting the app's documented "never a 500." Not triggerable by the normal write path (always valid JSON), only by out-of-band corruption; the codebase already uses the safe pattern elsewhere (`dedup.py`, `manifest_payloads`). **Fix:** Wrap `_detail` in try/except, log + skip/placeholder.

**Manifest upload buffers the whole request body with no size limit.** `palletscan/web/app.py:216-238` — `await request.body()` fully buffers before any check; an accidental multi-GB local upload can OOM the process running the live pipeline. Localhost-only, unauthenticated by design. **Fix:** Reject early on `Content-Length` above a small bound (manifests are KB) and/or cap bytes read via `request.stream()`.

**Reconciliation "true read rate" ignores the report time window.** `palletscan/reporting/manifest.py:51-54` — computed over all stored pass rows ever, while A/B `read_rate` is windowable; for a bounded trial, scans from a different shift still count as "matched." In the shipped dashboard both numbers are lifetime-wide (no window UI), so they're consistent by default; divergence needs a hand-built windowed A/B URL. **Fix:** Thread `window_from`/`window_to` into the reconciliation path and filter scanned rows by `wall_time_iso` (reuse `_in_window`).

**Other low-impact items (verified real, minimal owner impact):** consecutive-read-failure counter resets on any good frame, masking a chronically glitching device (`camera.py:394-403`); connect-verify discards ~1s of frames per reopen, widening the post-recovery blind window (`camera.py:257-267`); `_outage_start` resets on the first yielded frame, so a flapping camera never accumulates outage (`watchdog.py:167-169,237-239,323-332` — only matters if `max_outage_s` is enabled); lock fd leaked if `_write_holder()` fails after acquire (`instance_lock.py:121-122`); contender reads empty/partial holder diagnostics in the flock→write gap (`instance_lock.py:61-73,121-122`); holder JSON truncated at 4096 bytes for very long argv (`instance_lock.py:69,131-134`); a single failing variant future aborts the rest of the fallback for that frame (`decode_engine.py:220-225`); force-closing an overlapping open segment attributes the old segment's miss end-time to the new open instant (`pass_tracker.py:130-139`); full-res preview render before downscale wastes per-client CPU (`web/preview.py:77-119`); calibrate live-metrics first second can report low fps (`warmup_frames=0`) on AVFoundation (`calibrate.py:292-293`); A/B markdown table breakable by a `|`/newline in a source id (`render.py:52-67`); MJPEG `<img>` error handler retries a hard-404 forever at the same rate (`web/static/app.js:74-86`); a failed `/api/events` mislabels the dashboard "connection lost" even when `/stats.json` succeeded (`app.js:394-401`).

**One UI item worth a quick fix (verifier kept at Medium):** `refreshMisses` freezes the *entire* miss gallery while *any* note input is focused (`web/static/app.js:253-260`) — an operator who clicks a note field and steps away leaves the gallery frozen (new misses/other-client updates never render). Server data is unaffected; it self-corrects on blur. **Fix:** Rebuild only non-focused cards (the existing `noteDrafts` map already preserves typed text), or at minimum append newly-arrived miss cards.

---

## macOS-on-arrival risks

These are the platform-specific items the owner will hit *first* when plugging in the See3CAM cameras, in roughly the order encountered. All carry residual uncertainty until a real camera is attached — note them as "verify on first connect."

1. **First connect can hang hard (Finding 1).** `measure_achieved_fps` blocks in `cap.read()` with no timeout, and on the *first* live run signal handlers aren't installed yet — a wedged AVFoundation open/read may need SIGKILL. Fix this before plugging in.
2. **Native device-open can hang the whole station (Finding 14).** A wedged `cv2.VideoCapture(index, AVFOUNDATION)` during a reopen can't be interrupted by `close()`; in A/B mode it can hang the entire station with no supervisor recovery.
3. **Calibration may lock MJPG over a raw mode (Finding 11).** AVFoundation returns garbage for the FOURCC readback, so `choose_mode`'s uncompressed-over-MJPG preference no-ops — MJPG artifacts hurt Data Matrix decode. Verify the chosen fourcc on each camera; per ARRIVAL_CHECKLIST, confirm the readback is truthful.
4. **Geometry snapping → infinite reopen loop (Finding 4).** If AVFoundation snaps the configured 2064×1552/1920×1200 mode to something else, the watchdog reopens forever with no escalation and the A/B arm sits silently at zero. Watch `source.connect_mismatches` and the reconnect log on first connect.
5. **Exposure/gain controls largely no-op (by design, but verify).** AVFoundation ignores most UVC control props; the run path warns-and-continues. Run `calibrate`/`selftest` to see the "controls unverified" warning and `verify_exposure_effect` result — global-shutter blur budget depends on pinned exposure, which you can only truly validate on the Windows target.
6. **Memory pressure on a single dev box (Finding 9).** ~1.7 GB/camera of rolling-buffer evidence, ~2.5–3.4 GB in A/B mode, can page/OOM the dev machine and cause frame drops → missed accounting.
7. **No-frames-after-open scans nothing forever (Finding 5).** A device that opens/enumerates but never delivers frames leaves the supervisor reporting "healthy" while zero pallets are counted, because `max_outage_s` is off by default. Set `max_outage_s` before going live.
8. **Lower-priority macOS items:** an external/network/FUSE `--data-dir` produces a misleading "instance already held" exit 4 (Finding 6); the child log-prune can delete the open `supervisor.jsonl` after a multi-week quiet period; `import_pylibdmtx` at module import turns a missing libdmtx dylib into an import-time crash for synth/replay paths. The Y16 truncation (`video.py:76`) does **not** apply on the auto-calibrated GREY/`convert_rgb=True` path the 37CUGM will actually use.

**Confidence note:** Findings 1, 4, 5, 11, 13, 14 and the exposure-control behavior all depend on real AVFoundation read/open/negotiation semantics that were never exercised against hardware. The *code defects* are confirmed (timeouts missing, escalation valves off, readback-classification gap, joins unbounded); whether each manifests depends on how the See3CAM driver behaves on first connect. Treat the first bring-up as the test that confirms or clears the hardware-dependent half.
